title: 2026 03 25 - HE
displayTitle: 2026-03-25 - Health Committee - Briefing from the Prostate Cancer Foundation
topic: Briefing from the Prostate Cancer Foundation
description: 25 March 2026 | Health Committee Briefing from the Prostate Cancer Foundation Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora Karen Bartholomew, Chair, National Screening Advisory Committee Jane O’Hallaran, Deputy Chair, National Screening Advisory Committee
descriptionSummary: Briefing from the Prostate Cancer Foundation. Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora. Jane O’Hallaran, Deputy Chair, National Screening Advisory Committee

chunks.text: Good morning, Karen. Very good to have you here. Welcome to the Health Committee. We have you here discussing a part of a briefing from the Prostate Cancer Foundation, and we've received the response back from Health New Zealand on that. We have you in here for 10 minutes to talk to that.

If you're able to, leave us some time to answer questions, that would be appreciated by members of the committee. We will pass it over to you to make some opening remarks. Thank you. Thanks very much for your time. I'm Karen Bartholomew, public health physician and chair of the National Screening Advisory Committee, here with my colleague, Jane O'Halloran, who's deputy chair. Thanks for the opportunity to have a conversation today. And just before we start, I did really want to acknowledge that prostate cancer touches a large number of men and

their wider families, including my own. And really want to acknowledge the impact of that as we talk about prostate cancer screening in quite a technical way. You asked about the current state of prostate cancer screening in New Zealand. So PSA testing is widely available. The test itself is free, and it's part of a conversation in primary care about testing in that particular man's circumstances, and the benefits and harms of that.

And we actually have really high levels of testing, of PSA testing, in New Zealand, much higher than our comparator countries, probably about double Australia, our colleagues in Australia. So we have very high testing rates, but there is variation in testing. For example, Maori, Pacific, and Indian men have lower rates of testing, and there's lower rates of testing in some parts of New Zealand.

We have, I think, in New Zealand, an opportunity to really reduce the harms from PSA testing. I think those harms are widely recognized and understood, and we've had a number of conversations with the Prostate Foundation and with others about that. And to really rapidly learn, I think, from the broader international, particularly European and our Australian colleagues' approaches to optimizing the benefits and harms so that we can consider what that balance looks like and whether that tips in favor of a prostate cancer organized screening program as opposed to what we have now, which

is opportunistic testing. So I think there's a large number of trials and pilots happening internationally, and we have regularly reviewed, as NSAC, the evidence. We reviewed it in 2018 and '19, 2023, and again in '24, and we will be doing so this year because that evidence rapidly evolves. And we have had conversations with the Praise U pilot. I think you asked a question about that, with the Praise U pilot, and we'll continue to do so. The Praise U pilot actually completes this year in April, and they just had a big policy summit in

March, just the 4th of March, so a couple of weeks ago, where they presented back some of their early findings. And they are really starting to drill down on what a harmonized approach to reducing the harms and improving the benefits of prostate cancer screening is, and they're now moving on to look at cost-effectiveness. And we've also got the opportunity to learn from a big review in the UK, which is similarly looking at those things as they embark on a very, very large trial called Transform, where they're looking at quite specific

risk-based screening approaches and the introduction of new, better imaging modalities, such as biparametric or fast MRI scans. So it's really a time of change and evolution in this space and an opportunity for us to really understand what that might mean in our context. Excellent, Karen. Thank you very much. Dr. Verhaagh.

Thanks, Dr. Bartholomew. The paragraph in the letter you sent to the committee says that the National Screening Advisory Committee reviewed prostate cancer screening against eight criteria. Could you explain the criteria that weren't met for prostate cancer screening, so that we can understand why it is that it's considered potentially harmful?

Certainly. So the eight criteria are the standard New Zealand screening criteria. So we assess much more broadly than whether there's benefits or harms. There's a whole range of other criteria to consider. In particular, that assessment was of general population prostate cancer screening, and I think there's wide international agreement that general population, classic PSA testing and biopsy approaches are very unlikely ever to meet those screening criteria because of the balance of benefits and harms. So any future assessments would be done against

a risk-based screening approach. So Jane, did you want to talk about those two criteria that were met? Sorry, I just missed that, Karen. Which two are you talking about? The two criteria that were met in the last assessment we did of prostate screening. Well, I was just concentrating on the...

I mean, we do have a test, but it is not very sensitive nor specific. I mean, the key issue is around the harms with over-diagnosis and over-treatment, and what other ones were you thinking about, Karen? So the range of other criteria we consider are the cost-effectiveness or cost benefit- Yeah... and that's clearly not met, and that's been confirmed again in the large UK cost-effectiveness analysis just released in December 2025.

And they actually looked at quite a large number of those stratified approaches, and they're not yet cost-effective. But I think the opportunity with Praise U is that if there's refinement of those benefits and harms, it may become cost-effective for some of those strategies. So cost-effectiveness was definitely one that wasn't met, as was health capacity, health system capacity, and particularly, there's a number of other criteria about social and ethical issues in terms of informed consent.

Yeah. But we can provide a list of what that assessment is to the committee, if that's useful. So- Yeah. Next one. So just for clarification, so when the potential prostate screening program was evaluated, that was just based on a PSA test and no stratification, just on the PSA test?

And is that my understanding? Yes. The last evaluation that we did, that was what it was based on as most international programs similar to ours, international committees that provide advice on screening, similarly based on that. And I think from now on, as the opportunity, as the evidence comes out, to actually consider quite specific detail of risk-stratified approaches.

Risk-stratified approaches are much more complex with a large number of potential combinations that quite markedly affect the benefit and cost, and risk profile. And very much. And just following up there, but appreciate one of the concerns is over-treatment, and the adverse effects of that.

In the letter that you have provided, you go through numbers. Can you tell the committee how that kind of compares to, say, the breast cancer screening program? Mm-hmm. Yeah. The risk of over-diagnosis and over-treatment is substantially higher for prostate than any of the other comparable programs.

So the estimates of over-diagnosis range from about 40% to 70%. So that means that for every life saved, two to three prostate cancers are over-diagnosed. So that's quite a significant level of over-diagnosis. For example, the comparison in breast is the level in breast is approximately 20% over-diagnosis.

And the harms related to over-diagnosis in prostate are quite a different profile as well because of the harms with biopsies, which have particular set of harms, and then the surgery, radiotherapy, et cetera, have additional harms. So it's really the consequential harms, not just the quantum of over-diagnosis that's particularly problematic.

Sorry. Did you say 40, four, zero percent to 70%, seven, zero? Correct, over-diagnosed. So most cancers that are diagnosed with PSA testing are over-diagnosed, which is why in the treatment pathway, active surveillance, which is where those cancers are not treated, but they are surveilled over time.

Approximately 88% of prostate cancers in Australia and New Zealand are under active surveillance. Sorry, can I just have a- I'll come to Dr. Fletcher. So with stratification and additional imaging, you expect that number to decrease? Absolutely. MRI by itself reduces the harms, particularly related to biopsy.

So it reduces the number of biopsies needed and their consequent harms. And there are a number of risks calculators that are used in addition to MRI, and that also changes the profile. And there's quite a few options for risk calculators, and there's quite a few options for when those calculators are conducted prior to biopsy or after biopsy.

Thank you for your patience, Sasha. There's also advocacy that the bowel cancer screening age gets reduced and that new screening for former smokers for lung cancer gets introduced. Can you give an indication of the comparable cost efficacy of those interventions relative to prostate screening, please?

Thank you. Certainly, lung cancer screening, NSAC has assessed the evidence on lung cancer screening, and there is a very, very clear benefit to lung cancer screening, estimated to be a life saved a day from that program. And there's also clear cost-effectiveness for a lung cancer screening approach, which is a targeted screening program.

There's probably no other comparable screening program that has that level of benefit, and perhaps no other health intervention that has that level of benefit. So certainly from a potential new screening program introduction, lung cancer screening has a significant health benefit. And NSAC has certainly recommended and supported lung cancer screening.

In terms of bowel cancer screening, that similarly has a really significant benefit profile, and a large number of lives saved with extending age ranges. And we have modeling on that that supported age range extension decisions. NSAC has considered that quite specifically. You've been quite definitive in that answer.

So are you saying those are cost-saving across the health system and prostate screening is not, or are you saying they are more cost-effective, but none of them are cost-saving? I don't think any screening program, as far as I know, is cost-saving, but lung is probably the closest to that.

It is highly cost-effective. And prostate cancer screening, as far as we know, is not cost-effective. The UK evidence, even with MRI to reduce harms and even with risk stratified triage approaches, so PSA-based or age-based, is not cost-effective. In the UK, they've only said bracket testing is likely to be cost-effective.

Thank you, Dr. Bartholomew. And thank you, Jane, as well. Very good to have you here today. We've reached our time, but appreciate the responses that you've given us in this paper and to the committee today. Oh, Mr. Chair, just one other point. Dr. Allen. The officials mentioned the assessment against the eight criteria, if that could be shared with the- Yes, they did... the committee, please.
Thanks. Certainly. Thank you. Thanks. Have a good day. They do have shared outside. We'll move to private, please.
Thank you.
